1: Psalm 2 is a royal psalm likely used at the coronation of Davidic kings. Historical setting: at the accession of a new king, surrounding vassal states and subject peoples routinely tested the new ruler's strength by withholding tribute or openly rebelling. ANE royal inscriptions (Egyptian victory stelae, Assyrian annals) document this pattern regularly — 'When I ascended the throne, all the enemies conspired against me.' The Psalm reframes this standard political crisis as cosmic rebellion against YHWH: the nations' 'raging' (reash, tumult and conspiring) is not merely political instability but spiritual defiance.
2: 'Against his Anointed One (mashiach)' — the anointing ceremony (1 Samuel 10:1; Psalms 16:13; 1 Kings 1:39) was the public ritual designating the Davidic king as YHWH's chosen representative. Oil was poured on the king's head before witnesses, marking the transfer of divine appointment. To rebel against the mashiach was to rebel against YHWH's choice itself — a transgression beyond ordinary warfare. The conspirators' target is not the king as a political figure but the divine appointment he embodies; the Psalm frames political rebellion as theological revolt.
3: 'Let us tear apart their bonds and throw off their cords from us' — the suzerain-vassal treaty in the ancient Near East bound subjects through symbolic imagery of yoke and cords. The Mosaic covenant itself used yoke language (Leviticus 26:13: 'I have broken the bars of your yoke'; Jer 27-28: the yoke of Babylon). The rebel nations want to break the treaty obligation — the tribute, military service, and political submission owed to YHWH's king. This is not merely political rebellion but covenant-breaking: the rejection of the suzerain relationship that carries divine sanction.
4: 'He who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord scoffs at them' — divine laughter as cosmic derision (sachaq) is theologically striking; this is the only place in the Psalter where YHWH laughs. The absolute power disproportion between YHWH enthroned above the heavens and earthly conspirators makes the rebellion objectively absurd. In ANE royal propaganda, the great king sometimes expressed contempt for rebel threats in similar terms. The Psalm uses this register to assert that the greatest earthly coalition cannot constitute a real threat to the divine sovereign.
5: 'He will speak to them in his wrath and terrify them in his burning anger' — the divine word-as-judgment echoes the prophetic tradition: the spoken decree of YHWH creates the historical reality it announces (Isaiah 55:11). The Psalm anticipates the prophetic corpus where YHWH's speech against the nations is itself the instrument of their judgment (cf. Isa 13, the oracle against Babylon; Jer 46-51, oracles against the nations). The terror is not merely emotional but ontological: the divine word destabilizes what the rebels thought was secure.
6: 'I have installed my King on Zion, my holy hill' — David's capture of Jerusalem from the Jebusites (2 Samuel 5:6-9) and his subsequent bringing of the ark of the covenant to Zion (2 Sam 6) made Jerusalem simultaneously the political capital and the religious center of the Davidic kingdom. The choice of an unconquered city (belonging to no tribe) as capital was a masterstroke: Zion belonged to no single tribe and so could serve as the united monarchy's center. YHWH's identification of Zion as 'my holy hill' elevated the political decision into a theological claim: the eternal God had committed his presence to a particular geographic location.
7: 'The LORD's decree (choq)' is the royal charter, the formal establishment of the covenant relationship. The divine adoption formula — 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you' — had parallels in Egyptian royal ideology where the pharaoh was literally the son of the sun-god Re, and in Mesopotamian royal texts that described the king as nursed by divine goddesses. In Israel, this was covenantal rather than biological: the Davidic king became YHWH's representative son at enthronement (2 Samuel 7:14; Psalms 89:26-27). The 'today' of begetting is the day of enthronement — the moment of public divine designation.
8: 'Ask me and I will give the nations as your inheritance and the ends of the earth as your possession' — the terms of the Davidic covenant's imperial promise (2 Samuel 7:9: 'I will make your name great like the names of the greatest men'; Psalms 89:25-27). The 'rod of iron' (shebet barzel) is the royal scepter here exercised with irresistible authority. The subjugation of the nations as 'inheritance' (nachalah) uses the same vocabulary as Israel's inheritance of the land — the divine grant that established the covenant people in their territory. The promise extends the territorial logic of the Abrahamic and Mosaic covenants to a universal scale.
9: 'You shall rule them with an iron rod and shatter them like a clay vessel' — the image of breaking pottery (fragile, brittle) against an iron scepter (indestructible) comes from the standard ANE iconography of conquest: defeated enemies were depicted as broken vessels under the feet of the victor. Egyptian royal inscriptions list enemies' names on pottery shards that were then ritually shattered. The Psalm uses this imagery to convey the totality of divine authority over the nations: opposition to YHWH's king is as futile and temporary as pottery against iron.
10: 'Be wise (haskel), O kings; take warning, O rulers of the earth' — the Psalm shifts from oracle to instruction, adopting the register of wisdom literature (Proverbs). The call to wisdom is the call to see reality clearly: the Davidic king is YHWH's installed representative, and the appropriate response is submission, not rebellion. The surrounding kings are offered an alternative to destruction: covenant submission under YHWH's king is not defeat but wisdom. This appeal to the nations' rulers reflects the Psalter's universal scope — YHWH's covenant claims extend beyond Israel to all governing authorities.
11: 'Serve the LORD with reverent fear, and celebrate with trembling' — the paradox of joyful reverence: worship that integrates both delight and awe. This combination resists two errors: slavish fear without joy, and presumptuous celebration without reverence. The pairing anticipates NT formulations of the 'fear of the Lord' as the beginning of wisdom that does not preclude but rather grounds joy (Philippians 2:12: 'work out your salvation with fear and trembling'; 1 Peter 1:17).
12: 'Pay homage to the Son (nashqu-bar)' — the kiss as political submission gesture was standard in the ancient Near East: subject peoples kissed the feet or hem of the suzerain's garment; Samuel kissed Saul after anointing him (1 Samuel 10:1); the prodigal son's father ran and kissed him, a reversal of the submission dynamic. The Psalm ends with the same word as Psalms 1:1 — ashrê (blessed): 'Blessed are all who take refuge in him.' This canonical link is deliberate: the blessed person of Ps 1 who meditates on Torah is the same as the person of Ps 2 who takes refuge in YHWH's anointed king. Torah-keeping and covenant allegiance to the Davidic king are two expressions of the same fundamental orientation.
